reasoning: Following the fall of Bashar al-Assad in December 2024, the new Syrian interim government actively moved to expel Russian military forces from their strategic bases. By January 2025, de facto authorities annulled the long-term lease for the Tartus naval base and demanded an immediate Russian withdrawal, setting a reported deadline of February 20, 2025. Throughout January and February, verifiable satellite imagery and logistics tracking confirmed that an evacuation operation was well underway, with major warships exiting the Mediterranean and ro-ro cargo vessels (like the Sparta class) loading heavy equipment for return to Russia. Furthermore, Syrian forces were seen actively blocking Russian convoys from re-entering the port in February, demonstrating that the host authorities were enforcing the expulsion. Given that the evacuation was heavily advanced by mid-February, four months (up to June 2025) is an ample timeframe to conclude operations and withdraw all remaining personnel. The main residual risk is whether a handful of personnel remain in a diplomatic or caretaker capacity that could technically violate the "withdrawing all personnel" clause, or if Russia successfully bought time at Khmeimim/Tartus; however, the strong hostility of the new regime makes completely abandoning Tartus the overwhelmingly likely outcome.
